request.question: What was Debbie Rowe's husband dubbed?

title: Jackson v. Jackson
Lin Huiyin
Michael Jackson

List of The Millers episodes

sentences: Deborah Rowe–Jackson v. Michael Joseph Jackson settled the divorce of Michael Jackson and his second wife Debbie Rowe.
Full custody was granted to Jackson.
Rowe is allowed visitation rights.
She gets property in Palm Beach, Van Nuys and Miami.
Rowe alleged sexual abuse as mentioned in the last amendment in the divorce.
Lin Huiyin (; known as Phyllis Lin or Lin Whei-yin when in the United States; 10 June 1904 – 1 April 1955) was a noted 20th-century Chinese architect and writer.

She is known to be the first female architect in modern China and her husband the famed "Father of Modern Chinese Architecture" Liang Sicheng, both of whom worked as founders and faculty in the newly formed Architecture Department of Northeastern University in 1928 and, after 1949, as professors in Tsinghua University in Beijing.
Liang and Lin began restoration work on cultural heritage sites of China in the post-imperial Republican Era of China; a passion which she would pursue to the end of her life.
The American artist Maya Lin is her niece.
<ref name = "Rowe/Kuan">Peter G. Rowe, Seng Kuan, "Architectural Encounters With Essence and Form in Modern China", MIT Press, 2002, p.219, ISBN  </ref>
Michael Joseph Jackson (August 29, 1958 – June 25, 2009) was an American singer, songwriter, and dancer.
Dubbed the "King of Pop", he was one of the most popular entertainers in the world, and was the best-selling music artist at the time of his death.
Jackson's contributions to music, dance, and fashion along with his publicized personal life made him a global figure in popular culture for over four decades.
Deborah Jeanne Rowe (born December 6, 1958) is an American nurse known for her marriage to Michael Jackson, with whom she had two children.
She lives in Palmdale, California.

Debbie Purdy (4 May 1963 – 23 December 2014) was a British music journalist and political activist from Bradford, West Yorkshire, with primary progressive multiple sclerosis, notable for her challenge to the law in England and Wales as relates to assisted suicide.
On 20 September 2009, it was announced that guidelines on assisted suicide law would be published by the UK Government.

The guidelines for England and Wales "come after a legal battle won by Debbie Purdy", as "Law Lords accepted earlier this year that [Purdy] had a right to know whether her husband would be prosecuted if he helped her to travel abroad to commit suicide."
Buy Me is a television program that has aired on HGTV in the U.S. since 2005, and on HGTV Canada since 2003, where it is that cable network's most popular show.
It is also seen in Belgium and South Africa, either dubbed or with subtitles.
It is produced by Whalley-Abbey Media Holdings (WHAM), which is owned by Debbie Travis and her husband, and produced "Debbie Travis’ Facelift".
The Snarling Beasties were formed in 1986 by Debbie Isitt and Mark Kilmurry and were influenced by the theatre of Steven Berkoff.
After performing "East" by Berkoff, Isitt became the theatre company's writer and director.
Mark Kilmurry played the lead parts in her first plays, "Gangsters" (1988), "Punch & Judy", (1989), "Femme Fatal" (1990) and "The Woman Who Cooked Her Husband" (1992).
The theatre company was very successful and won many awards including the Time Out theatre award, The Perrier Pick of the Fringe (three times) and The Independent Theatre Award.
Their theatre mixed a cinematic style with total theatre and created physical, dynamic and naturalistic plays.
The company was very influential but disbanded in 1996 though Debbie and Mark are both very successful in their chosen creative roles and still plan to do more work in the future.
Gallery of Fear (2013) is a four-part horror anthology, directed by Alan Rowe Kelly and Anthony G. Summer.
Kelly also wrote the screenplay for the film based on a story by Doug Smith.
The film stars Debbie Rochon, Raine Brown, and Susan Adriensen.
The four segments of the anthology are titled "Critics Choice," "By Her Hand, She Draws You Down," "Down the Drain," and "A Far Cry from Home."
The film was distributed by Southpaw Pictures and Tiny Core Pictures.
"The Millers" is an American sitcom that was created by Greg Garcia.
The multi-camera series premiered on CBS as part of the 2013–14 American television season and aired from October 3, 2013 to July 18, 2015.
"The Millers" is set in Leesburg, Virginia where Nathan Miller is a local television news reporter.
His sister Debbie runs a combination yoga studio/vegan restaurant with her husband Adam, with whom she has a daughter named Mikayla.
Nathan does not have any children and often hangs out with Ray, his friend who also serves as a cameraman for the fictional television station at which Nathan works.
As the series begins, Nathan and Debbie's bickering parents, Tom and Carol Miller, are returning from Myrtle Beach.
Nathan informs them he divorced his wife, Janice, several months ago.
Tom responds by deciding to leave Carol after being married for forty-three years.
Tom moves in with Debbie and Adam while Carol moves in with Nathan, each driving their children crazy.
On March 13, 2014, "The Millers" was renewed for a second season, which premiered on October 20, 2014.
Paris-Michael Katherine Jackson (born April 3, 1998) is an American actress, activist, and model.
She is the second child and only daughter of Michael Jackson and Debbie Rowe.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Produce a single, concise, structured first-hop evidence report as the `summary`. The summary must do all of the following and only include content that helps determine what to fetch next:

- Enumerate concrete facts found in the provided passages. For each fact include the passage source label and either a very short quoted fragment (up to about 15 words) when a decisive short fact appears or a brief paraphrase otherwise. Keep each fact to one short line.

- Explicitly identify the bridge entity(ies) and any missing or ambiguous fact(s) that prevent answering the question from the given passages. If a name or identifier in the question corresponds to multiple candidate pages present in `passages`, list every candidate variant and mark the name as ambiguous; do not choose between them.

- Prescribe concrete, prioritized next-hop retrieval targets to resolve each missing or ambiguous element. For each target, specify which page(s) or disambiguation page to fetch and what exact section, keyword, or datum to extract (for example: specific field such as birth year, office held, list membership, dates, or the section heading to check). Prioritize targets (1, 2, 3...) when helpful.

- Only produce a final answer in `summary` if every required fact for every entity is present and unambiguous in the supplied passages; otherwise do not produce a final answer and focus on retrieval guidance.

Formatting and content constraints:
- Keep the summary concise (a short paragraph or a few short bullet-like lines).
- Produce only the single output field `summary` and nothing else.
- Preserve source-linked evidence; do not assert facts without a passage label.
- Do not invent facts or assume which ambiguous candidate is intended.
- Avoid copying long verbatim spans; use short quoted fragments when decisive and paraphrase otherwise.
- When an expected authoritative page is missing from `passages`, note that absence and include that page title as a next-hop retrieval target.

Input: fields `question` and `passages`. Output: one field `summary` meeting the requirements above.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

You are to produce exactly one compact second-hop retrieval query that targets the missing bridge or supporting evidence referenced by summary_1. Follow these operational rules when constructing the single `query` string:

- Use visible anchor tokens from `question` or `summary_1` (named works, titles, artist names, years or other identifiers only if present) and append a short retrieval cue naming the bridge sought (e.g., artist, album, recording, genre, season count, passage, supporting quote).
- Emit one concise line (one string assigned to `query`), roughly ≤ 10–12 words, formatted either as a short interrogative that names the anchors or as a compact keyword string (anchors + bridge keywords).
- If summary_1 explicitly requests a missing attribute or passage, emit the focused retrieval query rather than asking for clarification.
- Do not include answers, conclusions, enumerations, provenance notes, speculative language, or user-facing explanations; do not invent entities or add tokens that are not present in the inputs.
- Include year or other identifier tokens only if they appear in the provided inputs and help disambiguate.
- Keep wording retrieval-oriented and terse. Preserve the fallback that if retrieval using this query later fails, downstream steps may request clarification.

Output contract: produce exactly one field `query` whose value is the single compact retrieval query line described above.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single field only):
1. Merge the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into one compact integrated `summary` that preserves the primary answer name(s) and only includes passage-supported facts needed to justify or disambiguate that answer.
2. If `context` already contains a concise answer (a short sentence or a name), keep that content as the first sentence of `summary` when it is consistent with facts in `passages`. If `context` lacks a concise answer, create a concise first sentence that names the primary answer(s), using the canonical labels from `context` when available and otherwise from `passages`.
3. Expand the summary only when `passages` provide explicit, matching second-hop evidence about one or more named entities referenced in `context`. When expansion is appropriate, append at most one short supporting sentence per named entity. Each supporting sentence must begin with the entity's full name as shown in the inputs and paraphrase up to a few core attributes that are directly present in the `passages` (examples: nationality, a notable award/status, a current affiliation/team, a birth date, membership/partnership relation, or an explicit founding/formation fact).
4. Keep `summary` concise: typically 1–2 sentences for a single entity; allow up to 3 sentences only when multiple distinct named entities each require one supporting sentence. Prioritize the facts most directly necessary to answer the `question`.
5. Only assert facts that are directly supported by the provided `passages` or `context`. Do not introduce new entities, dates, awards, affiliations, roles, or causal claims that are not present in the inputs.
6. Prefer paraphrase over long verbatim copying. Use short quoted phrases only when the exact short phrase (roughly ≤10 words) appears in the `passages` and quoting preserves clarity.
7. If `context` and `passages` conflict, prefer facts that are explicitly present in the `passages`: update the concise answer to match passage-supported content rather than preserving a contradicted `context`.
8. If the retrieved `passages` do not supply explicit supporting evidence for named entities in `context`, keep the `summary` concise and do not expand or speculate.
9. Output exactly one field named `summary` whose value is the integrated post-retrieval summary text. Do not output additional fields, metadata, or commentary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Follow these operational rules when producing `answer`:
- Use only the content of `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2` as your evidential basis. Do not introduce new facts or external information.
- If both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, set `answer` to exactly one lowercase token: yes or no. The `answer` field must contain nothing else (no punctuation, capitalization, quotes, explanation, or additional text).
- Decide which token to emit by aggregating the visible summaries only. Treat `summary_2` as the later integrated evidence; do not let unresolved language in `summary_1` alone prevent emitting the single-token result if both summaries now unambiguously agree.
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, incomplete, hedged such that the relation is unresolved, or if the question is non-binary, do not apply the single-token rule; instead follow the agent's existing final-answer fallback behavior for unresolved or non-binary cases (which may produce a fuller response or an uncertainty signal).

Produce only the `answer` field as specified.

response.hop1_query: What was Debbie Rowe's husband dubbed?
hop1_docs: Buy Me | Buy Me is a television program that has aired on HGTV in the U.S. since 2005, and on HGTV Canada since 2003, where it is that cable network's most popular show.  It is also seen in Belgium and South Africa, either dubbed or with subtitles.  It is produced by Whalley-Abbey Media Holdings (WHAM), which is owned by Debbie Travis and her husband, and produced "Debbie Travis’ Facelift".

Debbie Burton | Debbie Burton was an American singer.  She is best known for dubbing the singing voice of the young Baby Jane Hudson (played by child actress Julie Allred) in the 1962 film "What Ever Happened to Baby Jane? ", singing the song "I've Written a Letter to Daddy".  Burton also sang a duet with Bette Davis, the rock and roll song "What Ever Happened to Baby Jane?" , written by Frank DeVol and Lukas Heller.  It was released as a promotional single, with Burton's rendition of "I've Written a Letter to Daddy" on the flipside.  An instrumental version of "What Ever Happened to Baby Jane?"  can be heard in the movie.

Betty Noyes | Elizabeth Noyes Hand (October 11, 1912 – December 24, 1987) was a singer and actress best known for dubbing two of Debbie Reynolds' numbers in the 1952 film "Singin' in the Rain."  Today, this is a well-known example of dubbing in a movie musical; ironically, Reynolds's character in "Singin' in the Rain" was supposedly dubbing for another character.

Jackson v. Jackson | Deborah Rowe–Jackson v. Michael Joseph Jackson settled the divorce of Michael Jackson and his second wife Debbie Rowe.  Full custody was granted to Jackson.  Rowe is allowed visitation rights.  She gets property in Palm Beach, Van Nuys and Miami.  Rowe alleged sexual abuse as mentioned in the last amendment in the divorce.
Debbie Rowe | Deborah Jeanne Rowe (born December 6, 1958) is an American nurse known for her marriage to Michael Jackson, with whom she had two children.  She lives in Palmdale, California.

Debbie Boyd | Debbie Boyd (born November, 1957 in Gainesville, Florida) is a Representative in the Florida House of Representatives.  She received her associate degree in building construction from Santa Fe Community College in 1990.  She resides in Newberry, Florida and lives with her husband Tommy Boyd.

Debbi Fields | Debbi Fields (born September 18, 1956) is the founder and spokesperson of Mrs. Fields Bakeries.  Additionally, she has written several cookbooks and makes cookies.  She currently lives in Nashville, Tennessee, with her husband, former Holiday Inn and Harrah's CEO Michael Rose.
response.summary_1:
- [5] "known for her marriage to Michael Jackson" — Debbie Rowe is married to Michael Jackson.  
- [4] Title/summary: "divorce of Michael Jackson and his second wife Debbie Rowe" — confirms spouse named Michael (Joseph) Jackson.  
- Bridge entity: Michael Jackson (Michael Joseph Jackson) — present and not ambiguous in the passages.  
- Missing fact: None of the supplied passages states what he was "dubbed" (no nickname/epithet appears).  

Next-hop retrieval targets (prioritized):
1) Fetch the Michael Jackson page (main biography). Extract the lead/intro and infobox lines and any sentence containing the word "dubbed", "nicknamed", "also known as", or "dubbed the" — specifically check for "King of Pop" or other epithets.  
2) Fetch the full "Debbie Rowe" page's Personal life/Marriage section (if not already complete) and search for any mention of her husband's epithet or nickname.  
3) If multiple epithets appear or wording conflicts, fetch authoritative third-party sources (e.g., Britannica, major news outlets) that state the epithet and its attribution; extract the exact quoted phrase "dubbed the '...'" or equivalent.  

No final answer given here because the required "dubbed" wording is absent from the supplied passages.
hop2_docs: The Original Soul of Michael Jackson | The Original Soul of Michael Jackson is a remix album of songs by Michael Jackson, recorded and published early in his solo career in the early 1970s and remixed in 1987.  While the album claims to 'contains music never before released', the only new song available was a re-dubbed cover of Edwin Starr's 1970 hit "Twenty-Five Miles", sung by the Jackson Five and solely credited to Michael.  The original recording of the song was included on the 2009 set "".  Some songs were newly edited on the album, with a newly dubbed version of "Dancing Machine". " Ain't No Sunshine" were also slightly different in the vocals and a modern drum machine were added to "Twenty-Five Miles".  "Melodie" was planned for a single in an effort to promote the record in the US, but was scrapped for a promo-only single "Twenty-Five Miles"/"Up On The Housetop".

Michael Jackson | Michael Joseph Jackson (August 29, 1958 – June 25, 2009) was an American singer, songwriter, and dancer.  Dubbed the "King of Pop", he was one of the most popular entertainers in the world, and was the best-selling music artist at the time of his death.  Jackson's contributions to music, dance, and fashion along with his publicized personal life made him a global figure in popular culture for over four decades.
Dub (nickname) | People nicknamed Dub or Dubs include:
King of Pop | King of Pop is an honorific nickname most commonly associated with American pop icon Michael Jackson but may also refer to:

Michael Jackson's This Is It (album) | Michael Jackson's This Is It (or simply This Is It) is a posthumous two-disc soundtrack album by American singer Michael Jackson.  Released by MJJ Music on October 26, 2009, "This Is It" features previously released music, as well as six previously unreleased recordings by Michael Jackson.  "This Is It" was released to coincide with the theatrical release of "Michael Jackson's This Is It", a concert film documenting Michael Jackson's rehearsals for the This Is It concert series at London's O2 Arena.  "This Is It" is the sixth album to be released by Sony and Motown/Universal since Michael Jackson's death in June 2009.

Easy Star All-Stars | Easy Star All-Stars is a reggae collective with a rotating roster of musicians and singers founded by the co-founders (Michael Goldwasser, Eric Smith, Lem Oppenheimer & Remy Gerstein) of New York City-based Easy Star Records in 1997.  The first original album by the band, released in 2003, was an interpretation of Pink Floyd's classic "The Dark Side of the Moon" entitled "Dub Side of the Moon"; the second was 2006's "Radiodread", a song-by-song cover of Radiohead's "OK Computer".  In April 2009, they released a cover of The Beatles' "Sgt. Pepper's Lonely Hearts Club Band" entitled "Easy Star's Lonely Hearts Dub Band".  In 2012, "Easy Star's Thrillah" was released, a cover of Michael Jackson's classic album.

Love Songs (Michael Jackson album) | Love Songs is a compilation album by American recording artist Michael Jackson.  It was released on January 15, 2002 by Motown Records as a part of "Motown's Love Songs" line.  The album contains 14 love songs and ballads Michael Jackson recorded, either by himself or with The Jackson 5, during his time on Motown in the early '70s.  In this case there are many hits of Michael (such as "Got to Be There"), or The Jackson 5 hits, such as "Who's Lovin' You,".  It also includes an unreleased version of The Jackson 5 hit, "I'll Be There", and the original mix of Michael's "Call on Me".
hop2_titles: The Original Soul of Michael Jackson
response.summary_2: Debbie Rowe is married to Michael Jackson. Michael Joseph Jackson was dubbed the "King of Pop."
response.answer: He was dubbed the "King of Pop."
